reasoning: The Lean signature correctly captures the classical theorem. `[CommRing O] [IsDomain O]` matches "integral domain". `eif` formalizes existence of a prime factorization for every nonzero ideal (as a multiset of prime ideals whose product equals the ideal), and `uif` formalizes uniqueness of such factorization. The restriction `f.prod ≠ ⊥` in `uif` is not a weakening: since Mathlib's `Ideal.IsPrime` allows `⊥` as a prime ideal in a domain, uniqueness over arbitrary multisets of prime ideals would fail trivially (e.g., `{⊥}` vs `{⊥, ⊥}` both have product `⊥`); the restriction to nonzero products precisely captures "unique factorization of nonzero ideals". Together, `eif` and `uif` are logically equivalent to "every nonzero ideal has a unique factorization into prime ideals" (the ⊥-free restriction is automatic because any prime factorization of a nonzero ideal cannot include `⊥` in an integral domain). The conclusion `IsDedekindDomain O` matches Mathlib's standard definition (Noetherian, dimension ≤ 1, integrally closed domain).
